title: 'Lord of the Rings': 9 Movie Changes That Didn't Work

blocks.content: Few fantasy works are as enduring as J.R.R. Tolkien's The Lord of the Rings. It's inspired countless fantasy novels over the decades, and even spawned multiple films and a prequel series on Prime Video. Peter Jackson's movie trilogy is widely lauded for its overall faithfulness to Tolkien's indelible work and revered as a universally beloved adaptation. It's a bona fide classic, to boot.

While there's plenty to love about Jackson's depiction of Tolkien's intricately detailed Middle-earth, Jackson made some deviations from the source material that simply don't work. These nine movie changes completely transform beloved book characters into something unrecognizable and include scenes that don't exist in the titular book. Let's dive into these changes and why they don't pan out.

Why You Should Trust Me: I've been covering fantasy and sci-fi for nine years as a freelance entertainment journalist, specifically in the realm of TV. My primary coverage on MovieWeb has been Game of Thrones-related, but I'm a sucker for anything with magic.
Faramir Is a Completely Different Character in the Movies

Peter Jackson's The Lord of the Rings movies significantly change Faramir's arc. In Tolkien's book, he's a noble character with a strong moral compass. He's quite resistant to the One Ring, even going so far as to declare that he wouldn't even pick it up on the side of the road. Faramir's more of a gentle yet steadfast fighter than the reluctant warrior Jackson makes him out to be. Tolkien's seminal work also shows why he would be a better Steward of Minas Tirith than his older brother, Boromir. Their father, Denethor II, clearly favored Boromir over Faramir, making Faramir's being passed over for a role he deserved all the more tragic. This change to his character just doesn't work in Jackson's adaptation. He's a different person here.
Movie Faramir also does something that Book Faramir would never do – whisk Sam, Frodo, and the One Ring off to Osgiliath...

In The Lord of the Rings: The Two Towers, Faramir takes Frodo, Sam, and the One Ring to Osgiliath while en route to deliver the Ring to Denethor II. Faramir really wants to earn his father's respect. An entranced Frodo almost gives the Ring to a Nazgûl flying overhead, but Sam saves the day. Sam's speech to Faramir about Boromir's death and the corruptive influence of the Ring is just what Faramir needs to change his mind. He eventually allows Frodo and Sam to continue their trek to Mordor.

The Osgiliath scene doesn't transpire in Tolkien's source material at all. Faramir kidnapping the Hobbits is very out of character. Book Faramir possessed the fortitude to resist the One Ring, but in the movies, he tries to take the Ring to his father before ultimately abandoning his mission. Everything he does here contradicts his literary counterpart.

Jackson's trilogy doesn't feature a certain merry fellow with a bright blue jacket and yellow boots. Tom Bombadil is an enigmatic figure who helps our Hobbits early in their journey. Jackson cut Bombadil to shorten the story, and I understand that. However, to play devil's advocate, removing Bombadil from the narrative robs audiences of an important aspect of Middle-earth. He's a primordial entity, the Eldest, having a command of the Old Forest. And since he's immune to the One Ring's corruption, Bombadil represents a Middle-earth unsullied, in a way. It could've been interesting, perhaps even inspirational, for the Hobbits to see someone unmoved by the Ring. Not everyone falls prey to it. I also would've loved to see the Barrow-downs, as it's yet another fascinating – and haunting – aspect of this universe.
A Different Characterization From His Book Counterpart

Aragorn's change in The Lord of the Rings trilogy might be the most egregious of them all. He was never a reluctant king in Tolkien's novel. Rather, Aragorn was immensely proud of his lineage and embraced his destiny. He's confident and ready to become king. The scenes with Arwen and Aragorn in Rivendell, in which he doubts himself, didn't happen in the book. Boromir also didn't slight him in the source material – they respect each other. Aragorn grabs his inheritance by the horns, as it were. Meanwhile, in the movies, Aragorn worries he'll fall prey to the same pressures as his ancestor, Isildur. Not to mention, Book Aragorn was carrying the shards of Narsil from the get-go and had it reforged prior to the Fellowship's departure, whereas Narsil isn't reforged until Return of the King.

We've seen plenty of instances in which a character is hesitant to step into the limelight, and we've also seen the opposite. I don't think there's anything prideful or egotistical about someone embracing their fate. Having Aragorn be the confident king he is in the book also doesn't need to change his story with Arwen, either.

I will tolerate no John Noble slander in this house. That said, Jackson's trilogy does Denethor II dirty. In Tolkien's source material, Denethor is a tragic figure but wise and imposing. He fights against despair to serve his people in Gondor. However, The Return of the King depicts Denethor as an overt, mustache-twirling villain who's already descended into madness. We really didn't need another antagonist – we have Gollum, and Sauron's watchful Eye atop Barad-dûr. The film strips Denethor of crucial nuance, reducing him to a madman.
The Decapitation of the Mouth of Sauron

The Mouth of Sauron is in the Extended Edition of The Return of the King. Aragorn decapitates Sauron's chief lieutenant and emissary, which feels incredibly out of character. Sure, Sauron's mouthpiece taunts Aragorn and Gandalf, but it feels like Jackson inserted the scene purely for shock value. In the novel, the Mouth of Sauron is a Black Númenórean as opposed to a deformed creature, and after Gandalf rejects his terms, he leaves. Aragorn's choice to kill an emissary before battle feels like a political and diplomatic no-no, and certainly not a move that his book counterpart would make.

In Return of the King, Frodo orders Sam to "go home" after Gollum tricks Frodo into believing that Sam consumed their lembas bread. This was inserted to isolate Frodo, so he's only with Gollum when entering Shelob's lair (but Sam isn't far behind). Thankfully, Sam doesn't heed his friend's words and saves Frodo from Shelob. The "go home" scene outside the tunnels of Cirith Ungol doesn't exist in the source material. Frodo and Sam are never separated, and Frodo never questions his best pal's unwavering loyalty. Frodo also mistrusts Gollum in the book.

Naturally, this addition creates conflict and tension between our favorite Hobbits. However, it undermines just how close they are in the book. Friendship is a central theme in The Lord of the Rings, and there's nothing wrong with keeping our Hobbits thick as thieves from beginning to end. (It's all for narrative drama, I get it, but – devil's advocate.)
Jackson's Trilogy Changes Their Role in Pelennor Fields

Are they really ghosts? The Army of the Dead plays a significant role in Return of the King. In the movie, they fight in the Battle of Pelennor Fields, obliterating Sauron's forces (and they can't be killed). They can destroy corporeal forms. However, in Tolkien's source material, the Dead simply terrify the Corsairs of Umbar, Sauron's human allies, into surrendering Pelargir. They didn't participate in the Battle of Pelennor Fields. Aragorn releases them after they fulfill their end of the bargain and utilizes the Corsair ships, along with tangible reinforcements, to win the battle. Here, they behave more like spirits.

Their movie depiction stirs up confusion regarding what they actually are. The rules of ghosts go out the window. How can they physically hurt people if they don't have a corporeal form? This change doesn't make much sense beyond "a ghost army looks cool."
He's More of a Poet in Tolkien's Book

Gimli serves as major comedic relief in the film franchise, but in Tolkien's book, he's quite dignified. Everyone's favorite Dwarf is also articulate and wise, with the soul of a poet and a warrior. He's a chivalrous knight. While there's nothing wrong with having comedic relief in some form or other, it would've been wonderful to see more of this side of Gimli, per Tolkien's creation. Merry and Pippin already have moments of levity, so it's not like Gimli is the only comedic relief in the movies. Justice for Gimli. We deserve more multifaceted, layered characters that run the gamut of the viewing experience, from comedy to drama and beyond.
What changes in The Lord of the Rings movies didn't work for you? Let us know in the comments.
